serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of yangnyeom sauce is approximately one tablespoon (15 g), used as a coating or dipping sauce for fried chicken or other dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Gochujang-based Korean seasoning sauce — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for gochujang (FDC ID 1048085), ketchup (FDC ID 11935), and sugar (FDC ID 19335), weighted by typical yangnyeom sauce formulation proportions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA FoodData Central entry exists for yangnyeom sauce as a named product. Values are estimated from a standard recipe formulation: approximately 35% gochujang, 30% ketchup, 20% sugar, 8% soy sauce, 4% garlic, 2% sesame oil, 1% rice vinegar. Macros weighted accordingly. This is a medium-confidence composite estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweet chili-based condiment sauces — analogous estimate based on GI values for gochujang and ketchup-type sauces; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed journal (Diabetes Care)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for yangnyeom sauce. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors: gochujang (fermented chili paste, GI ~40–50 per analogous fermented condiment data) and ketchup/tomato-based sauce (GI ~45–55 per Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables). Sugar content places GI at the upper end of the low range. Composite estimate of 55 is defensible and conservative. Available carbohydrate per 15 g serving = (44.0 g total carb - 1.5 g fibre) / 100 * 15 g = 6.375 g. GL per 15 g serving = (55 × 6.375 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~980 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit yangnyeom sauce intake, as a single 15 g serving contributes approximately 147 mg sodium, and typical restaurant portions may involve multiple servings, rapidly approaching or exceeding the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (~32 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~55)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should use yangnyeom sauce sparingly, as its high sugar content and low GI can still contribute to blood glucose elevation; a single tablespoon (15 g) provides approximately 4.8 g of sugar and a glycemic load of 3.5.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium and high added sugar content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, yangnyeom sauce poses moderate concern due to its high sodium content contributing to blood pressure elevation and its high added sugar content, which is associated with adverse lipid profiles; portion control to one small serving per meal is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yangnyeom sauce (양념 소스) is a Korean spicy-sweet condiment sauce most commonly used as a glaze or dipping sauce for Korean fried chicken (yangnyeom chicken). The sauce is characterised by its bold combination of heat from gochujang (Korean fermented chili paste), sweetness from sugar and ketchup, umami depth from soy sauce and garlic, and a subtle nuttiness from sesame oil. The texture is thick, glossy, and clingy, designed to coat fried foods evenly. Yangnyeom sauce is a processed condiment made by blending and cooking its components together, typically without animal-derived ingredients, making it suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets, though it is not gluten-free due to wheat in soy sauce and gochujang. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense relative to its serving size, with approximately 210 kcal per 100 g, dominated by carbohydrates (44 g per 100 g) and notably high in sugar (32 g per 100 g) and sodium (980 mg per 100 g). Clinically, it is of concern for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium and sugar load. In Singapore, yangnyeom sauce appears on menus at Japanese-Korean fusion restaurants and Korean fried chicken outlets, including Sukiya. It is served as a side condiment or coating sauce, typically in 15–30 g portions.
common_user_queries: What is yangnyeom sauce made of?
How many calories in yangnyeom sauce?
Is yangnyeom sauce high in sugar?

confidence_reason: No direct approved-source entry exists for yangnyeom sauce as a named product in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo, or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are estimated from a composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the primary ingredients (gochujang, ketchup, sugar, soy sauce, sesame oil) weighted by a standard recipe formulation. GI is an analogous estimate based on the closest comparable condiment data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data; no single approved-source entry for yangnyeom sauce exists. 2. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1 as these micronutrients are not reported in the USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level entries used for this composite estimate and cannot be reliably derived without a direct product analysis. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as none of the standard yangnyeom sauce ingredients contain industrial trans fats. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0; confirmed zero as all standard ingredients are plant-derived. 5. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous condiment data; no direct GI measurement for yangnyeom sauce was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. The input record listed cuisine as 'Japanese' but the food is a Korean condiment served at Sukiya (a Japanese beef bowl chain offering Korean-style menu items in Singapore); cuisine has been corrected to 'Korean' with region_of_origin 'Korea'. 7. Halal status marked false as gochujang and soy sauce may contain alcohol from fermentation; halal certification would need to be confirmed per specific product. 8. The food_id GD000946 is preserved from the input record.

Recalculated glycemic_load_per_serving to 3.5 based on available carbohydrate per serving (6.375g) and GI 55, and updated gi_source_match_notes and health_context guidance accordingly.
